text: The surgical video shows the left side end-pelvic fascia being opened and dissected cordially as part of the conventional nerve sparing technique.

Using the Hinotori video system, the surgeon preserves the endpelvic fascia on the right side. The detrusor apron is cut, and the bladder and prostate are divided. The urethral catheter is held by the third robotic arm while the posterior side is divided. The retrotrigonal layer is separated, and the bilateral vas deferens and seminal vesicles are grasped.

The surgeon performs conventional nerve sparing on the left side using Hem-o-lok clips to secure the lateral pedicle vessels. The Hinotori robotic arms, with their eight joints, facilitate smooth movements. On the right side, endopelvic fascia preservation nerve sparing is executed, with the anterolateral superficial vascular layer being cut to access the prostate surface. Hem-o-lok clips are applied to prevent bleeding during each incision.

The surgeon dissects the prostate from the posterior side to the right lateral plane, extending cordially to the apex. Electrocautery is avoided to prevent thermal damage to muscles and nerves. At the apex, prostatic muscles are stripped, and the deep dorsal venous complex is ligated and sutured with 3-0 monocryl. The urethra is cut, and the prostate is removed. The left side pelvic muscles are exposed in the prostate bed, while the right side remains covered by the end-pelvic fascia and vascular layers.
